tech.headline: South African Municipalities Under Recovering Electricity Costs
tech.summary: South African research indicates that most municipalities under recover electricity costs. The study finds that stakeholder views regarding rooftop solar are often misinformed, specifically the belief that municipal electricity revenue acts as a ‘cash cow,’ a claim that is not supported by system-wide data. The findings highlight a disconnect between perceived revenue impacts and actual data regarding municipal electricity costs.
tech.searchQuery: Most South African municipalities under recover el 2026

tech.headline: Tandem PV Announces 30.4% Efficient Perovskite/Silicon Module
tech.summary: Tandem PV has announced a 100 cm² mini-module achieving 30.4% efficiency, featuring a proprietary 4-terminal perovskite glass layer on a Maxeon IBC silicon solar cell. Currently in third-party certification, CEO Scott Wharton states this breakthrough enables full-size modules hitting 28% efficiency later this year, which will be aimed at the utility-scale market for solar energy production.

tech.headline: Trina Solar Unveils 907 W Tandem Module with 29.2% Efficiency
tech.summary: Trina Solar has announced a 907 W tandem solar module that achieved a full-panel conversion efficiency of 29.2%. The efficiency was verified in testing by Germany’s TÜV SÜD. Unlike laboratory-scale prototypes, this specific module is designed for mass production, marking a significant step in the commercial availability of high-efficiency tandem solar technology for the broader energy market.
tech.searchQuery: Trina Solar announces 907 W tandem solar module wi 2026

tech.headline: Mayfield Renewables Opens Registration for Third Education Summit
tech.summary: Mayfield Renewables will hold its third-annual Education Summit on Sept. 29-30, 2026, in Chicago. The event, located at the University of Chicago’s David Rubenstein Forum, will feature 16 workshops focusing on microgrid, storage, and commercial solar topics. The summit is designed to address a significant and often undervalued gap in education within the renewable energy sector.

learn.title: Carbon Markets Power Clean Energy
learn.intro: Carbon markets translate emissions reductions into tradable credits, creating a financial reward for clean power. By pricing carbon, they steer investors toward renewable projects and help nations meet climate goals while unlocking private capital for the energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Think of carbon credits as vouchers for clean air: each credit represents one tonne of CO₂ avoided. Companies buy them like grocery coupons to offset emissions, turning pollution reductions into a marketable commodity.
learn.c2_text: When a solar farm generates electricity without emitting CO₂, it earns credits that can be sold. This extra revenue lowers the project's payback period, making renewable builds financially attractive even in low‑price markets.
learn.c3_text: India’s CDM projects have issued over 100 million Certified Emission Reductions, funding wind farms in Gujarat and solar parks in Rajasthan, and supporting the country’s target of 500 GW renewable capacity by 2030.
learn.stat1: Global carbon market volume reached 5.5 Gt CO₂e in 2023
learn.stat2: IEA estimates carbon pricing could mobilise $1.5 trillion in clean‑energy investment by 2030
learn.stat3: India: 80 million tonnes CO₂e avoided via CDM projects (2022)
learn.stat4: Average carbon credit price: $25 per tonne in 2023

learn.title: Colour Code of Hydrogen
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s colour labels—grey, blue, green—describe how the gas is produced and its carbon impact. Understanding these differences is crucial for policy makers and investors aiming to build a low‑carbon hydrogen economy.
learn.c1_text: Grey hydrogen is made from natural gas via steam methane reforming, releasing CO₂ directly. It’s like burning gasoline to make fuel: cheap but carbon‑intensive, emitting about 10 kg CO₂ per kg H₂ produced.
learn.c2_text: Blue hydrogen follows the same process but adds carbon capture and storage (CCS). Capturing 90 % of CO₂ reduces emissions to roughly 1 kg per kg H₂, turning a polluter into a cleaner producer when CCS works well.
learn.c3_text: Green hydrogen is electrolyzed using renewable electricity, emitting zero CO₂. India’s National Hydrogen Mission targets 5 GW of green electrolyzers by 2030, leveraging its 250 GW solar capacity.
learn.stat2: Blue hydrogen accounts for 20 % of global production (2023)
learn.stat3: India: 1.2 GW of green electrolyzer capacity under construction (2024)
learn.stat4: Cost of green H₂ fell to $2.5 kg in 2024, down 30 % from 2022

learn.title: Green Bonds Fuel Climate Finance
learn.intro: Green bonds are debt instruments earmarked for environmentally beneficial projects, channeling billions into renewable energy. They give investors a transparent way to support the clean‑energy transition while meeting ESG goals.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a loan where the borrower promises to plant trees with the money. In a green bond, the issuer pledges the proceeds to specific climate projects, and third‑party verifiers certify the use of funds.
learn.c2_text: Green bond proceeds often lower financing costs for wind farms and solar parks. A 5‑year green bond can cut a project's weighted average cost of capital by 0.3‑0.5 percentage points, accelerating construction.
learn.c3_text: India issued its first sovereign green bond in 2015 and now has over $12 billion in green bond issuances, financing 8 GW of solar capacity and 3 GW of wind projects across the country.
learn.stat1: Global green bond issuance hit $517 bn in 2023
learn.stat2: IEA: green bonds could unlock $2.5 trillion for clean‑energy by 2030
learn.stat3: India: 45 green bonds listed on stock exchanges (2024)
learn.stat4: Average coupon reduction for green‑bond‑financed projects: 15 bps

learn.title: Microgrids Empower Villages
learn.intro: Microgrids are localized energy networks that can operate independently from the main grid, delivering reliable power to remote villages and enhancing resilience after disasters. They are key to universal electrification and climate adaptation.
learn.c1_text: Picture a neighborhood with its own mini‑grid: solar panels, battery storage, and a control system balance supply and demand. If the central grid fails, the microgrid “islands” and keeps lights on.
learn.c2_text: Typical setups combine solar PV (2‑5 kW per household), a lithium‑ion battery (10‑20 kWh), and a diesel backup for reliability. Smart controllers prioritize renewable power, reducing fuel use by up to 60 %.
learn.c3_text: In Rajasthan’s Bikaner district, a 1 MW solar‑battery microgrid now powers 600 villages, cutting diesel consumption by 1.2 million litres per year and providing 24/7 electricity for schools and clinics.
learn.stat1: US DOE: over 1,200 microgrid projects deployed worldwide (2023)
learn.stat2: Microgrids can reduce outage duration by 80 % in disaster zones
learn.stat3: India: 5 GW of microgrid capacity planned by 2030
learn.stat4: Average cost of solar‑battery microgrid: $1,200 per kW installed

learn.title: Understanding LCOE Comparisons
learn.intro: The Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) expresses the per‑megawatt‑hour cost of generating electricity over a plant’s lifetime, allowing fair comparison of diverse technologies and guiding investment decisions.
learn.c1_text: LCOE = (total lifetime costs ÷ total lifetime electricity output). Imagine spreading the price of a car over all the miles you’ll drive; similarly, LCOE spreads capital, O&M, fuel and financing over generated MWh.
learn.c2_text: Key drivers include capital cost, capacity factor, discount rate, and O&M. For solar, a higher capacity factor (e.g., 22 % in desert sites) dramatically lowers LCOE, while low‑cost financing cuts the discount rate.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023 solar LCOE fell to $0.018/kWh, undercutting new coal plants at $0.05/kWh. This price advantage spurred 30 GW of solar auctions in 2024, accelerating the nation’s renewable target.

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge
learn.intro: High solar penetration creates a “duck curve” where midday generation overshadows demand, then a steep ramp at sunset strains the grid. Managing this shape is essential for reliable, low‑carbon power systems.
learn.c1_text: Picture a graph of net load (demand minus solar). Midday, solar supplies most power, flattening the curve—forming the duck’s belly. As the sun sets, demand spikes, creating the steep beak‑like ramp that operators must meet quickly.
learn.c2_text: Flexibility tools—battery storage, demand‑response, and fast‑ramping gas turbines—smooth the ramp. For every 1 GW of solar added, about 0.3 GW of storage or flexible load is needed to keep the beak manageable.
learn.c3_text: In Gujarat, 5 GW of solar caused a net‑load dip of 2 GW at noon, requiring 1 GW of battery storage to limit the evening ramp to 5 %/min, aligning with state grid codes.
learn.stat1: California’s duck‑curve ramp reached 12 GW in 2022
learn.stat2: US DOE: 10 GW of utility‑scale batteries installed by 2023 to address duck curves
learn.stat3: India: 30 GW of solar added in 2023 increased midday net‑load dip by 4 GW
learn.stat4: Each 1 GW of battery can shave 0.8 GW of ramp in high‑solar regions
